title: Type 1 Diabetes with DKA in Child
chief_complaint: My son has been drinking water nonstop, peeing every hour including through the night, and lost 8 pounds in 2 weeks. Now he's vomiting, has belly pain, and is breathing really fast and deep. He seems confused.

physical_exam.General: Lethargic, confused (GCS 13). Kussmaul respirations (deep, rapid breathing). Fruity breath odor.
physical_exam.Skin: Dry mucous membranes, poor skin turgor, sunken eyes (10% dehydration).
physical_exam.Abdomen: Diffuse tenderness (mimics acute abdomen - common in pediatric DKA). No rebound.
physical_exam.Neurologic: Confused. No focal deficits. Fundoscopy normal (cerebral edema risk - monitor).

hidden_data.abg: pH 6.95, PaCO2 18, HCO3 4, PaO2 98. Severe metabolic acidosis (DKA)
hidden_data.cmp: Na 125 (corrected Na 132 - pseudohyponatremia from hyperglycemia), K+ 5.8, HCO3 4, BUN 35, Cr 1.2, Glucose 520, Anion gap 28
hidden_data.gad_ab: GAD65 antibodies positive (autoimmune T1DM). IA-2 antibodies positive.

correct_diagnosis: New-Onset Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus with Severe Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA) - pH 6.95
key_learning_points: T1DM = autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells. Peak age 4-6 and 10-14 years. Often presents with DKA at diagnosis in children (30-40% in US, higher in developing countries)

Classic: Polyuria + Polydipsia + Weight loss (catabolic state). DKA: Vomiting, abdominal pain, Kussmaul respirations, altered mental status. Abdominal pain can mimic acute abdomen - resolves with DKA treatment

Pediatric DKA differs from adult: HIGHER RISK OF CEREBRAL EDEMA (0.5-1%, mortality 25%). Risk factors: Age <5, new-onset T1DM, severe acidosis (pH <7.1), low PaCO2, high BUN at presentation, rapid fluid administration, bicarbonate use

Pediatric DKA protocol: 1) Fluids: Initial bolus 10-20mL/kg NS over 1-2h ONLY if shock. Otherwise start maintenance + deficit over 48h (SLOW rehydration - reduces cerebral edema risk). 2) Insulin: 0.05-0.1 U/kg/hr IV (start 1h after fluids). 3) When glucose <250, add D5 to fluids (not D10 - pediatric protocol). 4) K+ replacement when K+ <5.3 and UOP established. 5) NEVER give bicarbonate (increases cerebral edema risk in children). 6) Hourly neuro checks

Transition to subcutaneous: When pH >7.3, HCO3 >15, anion gap <12, tolerating PO. Give first SC insulin dose 30min before stopping IV insulin. Insulin regimen: Total daily dose 0.5-1.0 U/kg/day, 50% basal (Glargine) + 50% bolus divided pre-meals (Lispro/Aspart). Education: Carb counting, sick day rules, glucagon for severe hypoglycemia, ketone testing. Follow-up endocrinology within 1 week
